data.title: Gold tumbles in 'buy the rumor, sell the news' profit-taking following Trump tariffs
data.description:
Gold futures trade lower Thursday but have moved off session lows, despite the general risk-off sentiment in the broader investment market triggered by President Trump's tariff announcement that initially sparked gains to new record highs of ~$3,170/oz overnight.

Traders attributed the dip to profit-taking and margin calls in other asset classes likely prompting investors selling some of their gold holdings to cover losses.

The tariffs have caused a sharp slide in financial markets because of concerns they could dampen economic growth, but many analysts belive gold's overall upward trajectory remains intact.

Most-active Comex gold futures (XAUUSD:CUR) for June delivery -1.3% to $3,124.20/oz, and May Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) -7.6% at $32.01/oz. 

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

Gold's Thursday price action is a classic "buy the rumor, sell the fact" scenario, Kitco.com analyst Jim Wyckoff said, and "with the marketplace un-nerved at present, for many traders it's a case of 'If you can’t sell what you want, you sell what you can.' That's likely in part what's causing safe-haven gold and silver markets to sell off, despite the risk-off general marketplace."

"People were selling profitable positions to cover those margins, but I think in the long run they'll continue to look for safe-havens and gold is certainly that," according to Zaner Metals senior metals strategist Peter Grant.

data.title: Fed Vice Chair Jefferson sees higher uncertainty to economic outlook
data.description:
The labor market is solid, U.S.economic growth has continued to expand, and inflation, while down from its peak, has recently been moving sideways, Federal Reserve Vice Chair Philip Jefferson said on Thursday.

"Significant changes in trade, immigration, fiscal, and regulatory policies currently are in process," he said, according to prepared text for the speech. "It will be crucial to evaluate the cumulative effect of these policy changes as we assess the economy and consider the path of monetary policy."

"In the current environment, I attach a higher degree of uncertainty to my projections than usual," he added.

"Labor market conditions are solid, and inflation has come down, though it remains somewhat elevated relative to our 2% goal," he said. "While the economy is in a solid position, surveys of consumers and businesses show heightened uncertainty about the economic outlook."

While GDP has been expanding  — at a 2.4% annual rate in Q4 — consumer spending may be waning. The "readings at the start of this year suggest less support for growth from household spending in the first quarter," he said.

Inflation, though down from its recent peak, appears to be "moving sideways." Complicating the picture, "the prospect of tariffs has consumers and businesses reporting that they expect higher inflation in the near term," Jefferson said. However, most measures of longer-term inflation expectation "remain consistent with our 2% inflation goal."

During his speech, Jefferson gave no indication of whether he expects the Fed to cut interest rates further. Like his Federal Open Market Committee colleagues, Jefferson emphasized that the FOMC will base its decisions on incoming data and that the central bank's monetary policy is not on a pre-set course.

Developing... check back for updates.

data.title: Canadian PM hits U.S. auto sector with retaliatory tariffs
data.description:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said the country will impose a 25% retaliatory tariff on all vehicles manufactured in the U.S. that don’t comply with USMCA standards or have non-Canadian content of USMCA-compliant vehicles from the U.S.

In acknowledgment of the “integrated production system” between the U.S. and Canada, import tariffs will not apply to auto parts, nor will Canadian tariffs impact imports from Mexico.

To avoid an escalation of U.S. trade policy, Carney’s proposals are meant solely to match restrictions placed by the U.S.

“Every single dollar raised — about $8 billion (before remission) — from these tariffs will go directly to our autoworkers and the companies affected by these tariffs," Carney was quoted as saying. "That’s on top of the $2 billion fund to build a new Made-in-Canada auto sector I announced a new Liberal government would implement.”

In related news, in an attempt to reverse President Trump’s emergency declaration against Canada, the U.S. Senate approved a resolution that would block tariffs against Canadian imports by a vote of 51 to 48 with four Republicans voting in favor of the resolution. While the measure has little chance of passing in the House, it will inevitably be vetoed by the president.

data.title: Lindsay Corporation targets fiscal 2025 growth in international irrigation and infrastructure segments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lindsay Corporation (LNN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Randy Wood highlighted record quarterly net earnings and revenue growth across both business segments, emphasizing strength in international irrigation markets and the completion of a $20M Road Zipper project in the Northeast.
  • Management noted stable performance in the domestic irrigation market, while international markets, particularly in the MENA region, drove growth. A $20M portion of a large MENA project was delivered this quarter.
  • Infrastructure segment saw strong results with operating income more than tripling year-over-year, supported by favorable revenue mix and increased Road Zipper sales.
  • CFO Brian Ketcham stated, "Consolidated revenues for the quarter increased 23% to $187.1 million compared to $151.5 million in the prior year, and net earnings rose 47% to $26.6 million or $2.44 per diluted share."

Outlook

  • Management expects stable demand for irrigation equipment in North America for the second half of fiscal 2025, with no meaningful near-term improvement in market conditions.
  • International irrigation markets are anticipated to continue growing, particularly in the MENA region, driven by food security and water conservation priorities.
  • Infrastructure growth is projected to remain strong for the full year, though timing of large Road Zipper projects remains uncertain.
  • The company is managing potential cost increases from recently announced tariffs through strategic supply chain measures and anticipates only marginal impacts to costs.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenues for Q2 2025 reached $187.1 million, up 23% year-over-year.
  • Irrigation segment revenues increased 11% to $148.1 million, with international irrigation revenues rising 42% to $71 million, offsetting a 7% decline in North America irrigation revenues.
  • Infrastructure segment revenues doubled to $38.9 million, driven predominantly by the completion of a major Road Zipper project.
  • Operating margins for the infrastructure segment improved to 34.1%, compared to 19% in the prior year, reflecting higher revenues and favorable margin mix.

Q&A

  • Brian Drab, William Blair: Questioned the recognition of international irrigation revenue and the future outlook for the MENA project. CFO Ketcham clarified that the quarter's revenue exceeded expectations but the cadence for subsequent quarters remains consistent with prior projections.
  • Ryan Connors, Northcoast Research Partners: Asked about irrigation segment margins and impacts of tariffs. CFO Ketcham explained that North American margins remained stable, while international project revenues provided volume leverage despite some gross margin dilution.
  • Adam Farley, Stifel: Inquired about pricing impacts on the domestic irrigation market. CFO Ketcham stated that while pricing actions have been implemented to offset steel cost increases, the impact on margins has been neutral.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on tariff impacts and the sustainability of international project contributions.
  • Management demonstrated confidence during prepared remarks, but their tone became more measured when discussing tariff uncertainties and domestic market challenges.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management displayed a slightly more cautious outlook, particularly regarding North American irrigation and tariff implications.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 2025 saw stronger international irrigation revenue growth compared to Q1. Infrastructure performance also improved significantly, primarily due to the execution of the large Road Zipper project.
  • Management’s tone shifted slightly toward caution in Q2, reflecting increased concerns over tariffs and continued headwinds in domestic markets.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused more on tariff impacts and international project sustainability, while Q1 discussions highlighted early indicators of market stabilization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged potential headwinds from rising interest rates and a challenging credit environment in Brazil.
  • Tariff uncertainties were emphasized as a key risk, with potential impacts on cost structures and agricultural exports.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the timing of large infrastructure projects and the ability of domestic markets to absorb further pricing actions.

Final Takeaway

Lindsay Corporation achieved record quarterly earnings and revenue growth in Q2 2025, driven by strong international irrigation markets and the successful execution of a major Road Zipper project. While management is optimistic about sustained growth in international markets and infrastructure segments, uncertainties surrounding tariffs and domestic market challenges present ongoing risks. The company’s strategic supply chain measures and global footprint position it to navigate these headwinds effectively.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Goodyear Tire soars as investors search for stocks without significant tariff exposure
data.description:
Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company (NASDAQ:GT) spiked in early afternoon trading on Thursday as investors latched on to the tire stock as a safe haven from tariff turmoil.

The Akron-based company has a strong manufacturing presence in the United States, with minimal reliance on production from Mexico and Canada. That domestic advantage is seen as protecting the company from an escalating trade war.

Earlier in the week, Deutsche Bank upgraded Goodyear Tire (NASDAQ:GT) to a Buy rating and upped its price target to $13. The firm is confident that Goodyear Tire (GT) is on track to achieve its $1.5 billion in cost savings and margin improvements by the end of 2026.

Shares of Goodyear Tire (GT) were up 14.3% to $10.43 vs. the 52-week range of $7.27 to $13.78. The gain was one of the largest seen in the consumer sector amid broad market declines. Notably, the tire stock has a clean sweep of Buy ratings from Seeking Alpha analysts, Wall Street analysts, and the Seeking Alpha Quant Rating.

Elsewhere in the tire sector, Michelin (OTCPK:MGDDF) was up 4.9%.

data.title: Stryker, Boston Scientific down as new tariffs impact medtech industry
data.description:
The new tariffs announced by President Donald Trump Wednesday afternoon are weighing on some top names in the medtech industry, including Boston Scientific (NYSE:BSX), Stryker (NYSE:SYK), and Medtronic (NYSE:MDT). 

Tariffs will impact medical devices depending on where the products are manufactured.

Among the worst hit names are GE HealthCare Technologies (NASDAQ:GEHC), Germany-based Siemens Healthineers (OTCPK:SMMNY), NeuroPace (NASDAQ:NPCE), Outset Medical (NASDAQ:OM), Delcath Systems (NASDAQ:DCTH), Massimo Group (MSMO), Insulet (NASDAQ:PODD), and Pro-Dex (NASDAQ:PDEX).

Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT) and Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ), two major medtech companies, are trading higher.

While the U.S. remains a major center for device manufacturing, Europe -- and especially Germany -- is also a manufacturing powerhouse. China is also growing as device exporter.

Under Trump's new tariff plan, imports from the European Union will face a 20% tariff. Those from China, 34%.

AdvaMed, the biggest medical device organization in the world, shunned the tariffs.

"If implemented as proposed, broad-based tariffs of this nature would act much as an excise tax," CEO Scott Whitaker said. "It will have a negative impact on innovation, cost jobs, and increase overall costs to the health care system. Historically, industries with a meaningful humanitarian mission have been exempted from broad tariffs, and as a result we have seen no to low tariffs on medtech from all key trading partners."

data.title: Nasdaq sinks 4%, Dow loses 1,100 points as tariffs spike up trade-war worries
data.description:
U.S. stocks suffered sharp losses Thursday afternoon, sliding down into correction territory as President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariffs stoked fears of a global trade war. 

Wall Street’s major indexes were off session lows but remained deeply in the red. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) -4.5%, and the Dow (DJI) fell more than 1,110 points, or down -2.8%. The S&P 500 (SP500) -3.4%, to 5,476.86.

The benchmark (SP500) early Thursday had erased ~$1.7T from its market capitalization, dropping to ~$47.73T, according to Capital IQ data. Trump late Wednesday detailed new tariffs that the U.S. is enacting on imports. The minimum baseline tariff will be 10%.

“Markets, caught off guard by the severity of Trump’s tariffs, reacted dramatically,” Jacob Falkencrone, global head of investment strategy at Saxo Bank, said Thursday. See the full list of U.S. tariff rates and the countries targeted.

“The immediate concern is the US economy, where the average effective tariff rate is jumping nearly 19 percentage points. That’s a direct tax on consumption and corporate costs, especially for industries relying on imported materials,” Falkencrone said. “This isn’t just a US-centric problem - it’s a potential global slowdown in the making.”`

Investors sought safety in U.S. debt, driving prices higher and the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) down 15 basis points, to 4.05%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 12 basis points to 3.73%. 

“If trading partners respond with equal retaliatory force, reciprocal tariffs could hit current S&P 500 operating income by 32%,” Savita Subramanian, head of Bank of America’s U.S. equity strategy, said. 

Canada will put a 25% tariff on vehicles imported from the U.S. that are not compliant with the trade deal between Canada, the U.S., and Mexico, Canada's Prime Minister Mark Carney said Thursday. 

The Office of the U.S. Trade Representative (USTR) said the reciprocal tariffs were calculated as the tariff rate “necessary to balance bilateral trade deficits” between the U.S. and its trading partners. 

“This was the worst-case scenario for tariffs and were not priced-into the markets, which is why we are seeing such a risk-off reaction,” Sanctuary Wealth’s Chief Investment Strategist Mary Ann Bartels said about stocks.  

Ten of the 11 sectors in the S&P 500 (SP500) fell, led by a -6% drop for Energy (XLE) as oil prices (CL1:COM) tumbled -7%. Consumer Staples (XLP) bucked the losing trend, +1.3%. 

Widely watched exchange-traded funds tracking key market indexes were hit. The Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO) -3.5%, the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) -4%, and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) -3.5%. 

Traders ramped up bets that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its May meeting, and odds for a back-to-back May-June quarter-point cut rose. 

“The current lack of trade reciprocity, demonstrated by our chronic trade deficit, has weakened our economic and national security,” US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said in a Wednesday statement on Trump’s tariff plan.

data.title: First Solar shines but most solar names fall sharply as tariffs hit southeast Asia supply
data.description:
First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) +3.5% in Thursday's trading but most solar shares trade lower after President Trump unveiled sweeping tariffs on imports to the U.S., including heavy levies on countries that are major manufacturers of solar products.

Tariffs on southeast Asia countries, as well as further action against Chinese products, could have the most obvious impact on the U.S. solar industry, with Vietnam slapped with a 46% tariff, Thailand suffers a 36% hit, Malaysia gets 24% and Cambodia is hit with a 49% tariff, as well as a further 34% levy on Chinese goods.

First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) rises as the only U.S.-headquartered company among the world's largest solar manufacturers, but other leading solar and alternative energy stocks are posting big losses, including Enphase Energy (ENPH) -7.9%, Solaredge Technologies (SEDG) -8.8%, Sunrun (RUN) -10.7%, Array Technologies (ARRY) -10%, Canadian Solar (CSIQ) -5.2%, Shoals Technologies (SHLS) -7%, Sunnova Energy (NOVA) -5.5%, JinkoSolar (JKS) -4.9%, Daqo New Energy (DQ) -9.9%, Bloom Energy (BE) -8.2%, Plug Power (PLUG) -6.4%.

ETFs: (TAN), (ICLN), (QCLN), (PBW), (PBD), (ACES), (CNRG), (ERTH), (SMOG)

The current anti-dumping and countervailing duty tariffs which have raised the price of solar cells entering the country from the southeast Asian countries already have reduced the volume of imports compared with 2024, according to the U.S. Department of Commerce.

Southeast Asian countries and South Korea still account for the overwhelming majority of solar cells entering the U.S. to supply its growing module manufacturing industry, which may now become more expensive, PV-Tech.org reports.

While the U.S. has established ~50 GW of PV module manufacturing capacity, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association, the components needed to supply domestic factories are still mostly imported from Southeast Asia, with cell production lagging significantly behind module manufacturing capacity.

data.title: Small Cap ETFs get clobbered as Trump’s tariffs rattle markets
data.description:
Wall Street's major indices plunged into the red on Thursday, with investors reacting to President Trump's latest tariff announcement, which has sent shockwaves through the market. While the technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has bore the brunt of the losses, small-cap exchange traded funds fared even worse, sinking to even lower levels.

Below, Seeking Alpha highlighted the 10 largest small-cap ETFs by market capitalization, detailing their rankings and specific price movements for the day.

See how each fund is trading on Thursday below:
